basmala: In the Name of God, the Infinitely Merciful, the Most Merciful.
intro: Al-Fīl — “The Elephant” — recalls an event famous in Arab memory: the year 570, the very year of the Prophet's birth. Abraha, the Abyssinian governor of Yemen, marched on Mecca with an army and an elephant to tear down the Kaʿba. But the elephant knelt at the gates of the Sanctuary and refused to move. And God sent swarms of birds bearing stones of baked clay, which destroyed the army. Tustarī gives the sense of the story: “Do you not know how your Lord dealt with your enemies while you were not yet in this world? In the same way He will deal with your enemies while you are among them, and will turn from you their cunning (makr, مَكْر).” And Qushayrī adds the most secret lesson: it is reported that ʿAbd al-Muṭṭalib, the Prophet's grandfather, still a pagan, went to Abraha to recover his camels seized as booty; when Abraha wondered that he did not plead for the Kaʿba, he answered: “I am the master of the camels; as for the House, it has its Master who will defend it.” And Qushayrī concludes: “If he was heard, it is because he asked nothing for himself — he asked for the House. And what is for God does not perish.” The sūra, beneath the historical episode, names a law: what is asked for God alone is answered.

versets.ar: أَلَمْ تَرَ كَيْفَ فَعَلَ رَبُّكَ بِأَصْحَابِ الْفِيلِ
versets.tr: a-lam tara kayfa faʿala rabbuka bi-aṣḥābi l-fīl
versets.trad: Have you not seen how your Lord dealt with the masters of the elephant?
versets.commentaire: a-lam tara (أَلَمْ تَرَ), “have you not seen” — God addresses the soul that knows, that has heard the story told. The question does not call for an answer in words but for a return of the gaze: remember what your Lord can do. Tustarī gives the bearing for the servant: “In the same way He will deal with your enemies while you are among them, and will turn from you their cunning.” What was done in the past is also a promise for the present — for one who truly entrusts himself.

mots.def: negative particle: negates the apocopated imperfect, the equivalent of a past negative.
raʾā: to see; in the apocopated imperfect after lam.
aṣḥāb: the people of, the masters of.
fīl: the elephant — an animal then unknown to Arabia.

versets.trad: Did He not make their scheming go astray,
versets.commentaire: kayd (كَيْد), cunning; taḍlīl (تَضْلِيل), going astray. Tustarī briefly: “Their scheme, in its annulment.” And Qushayrī specifies — their plan was carried to its very end, and there, at its accomplishment, it turned upon itself. It is a spiritual law often repeated in the Quran: God does not outwardly oppose evil — He lets evil run to its end, and at the end of the road it is found empty. Every scheme against the Light, in the long run, leads itself astray.

mots.def: jaʿala: to make, to render, to place.
mots.gram: apocopated imperfect verb, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: taḍlīl: the leading astray, the falling into error.

versets.trad: and send against them birds in swarms,
versets.commentaire: ṭayran abābīl (طَيْرًا أَبَابِيلَ). Qushayrī, briefly: “abābīl means: gathered and scattered.” The birds come in groups and in singles, in successive bands. The Sufi reading may hear: it is not the most visible forces that undo the armies of evil. What comes from on high comes by hidden ways, sometimes in humble forms, sometimes through things one would not have believed capable. Providence uses birds, and small stones.

mots.def: preposition ʿalā + plural suffix pronoun: upon them, against them.
abābīl: rare word (hapax) of the Quran; according to Qushayrī, “gathered and scattered” — in successive bands, in varied bands.

versets.trad: pelting them with stones of baked clay.
versets.commentaire: sijjīl (سِجِّيل), according to Qushayrī, comes from the Persian sang-gil — “stone-clay”, baked clay like brick. The most powerful of Arabia's beasts — the elephant, never seen before — is undone by the smallest of weapons: pebbles carried by birds. A reading reversing every logic of force: what saves the House of God is not a deployment of means to match the attack; it is, on the contrary, the smallness of what God chooses for His defence — that one may not be mistaken about Who acts behind it.

mots.gram: imperfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. fem. sing.

mots.def: preposition: from, of; indicates the material.
sijjīl: stone of baked clay; according to Qushayrī, a Persian composition (sang-gil, “stone-clay”).

versets.trad: He made them like devoured husks.
versets.commentaire: ʿaṣf maʾkūl (عَصْف مَأْكُول): “the tips of the seedlings before they ripen, eaten through”, says Qushayrī. The image is not only defeat — it is defeat before maturity: wheat that has not even had time to ripen, broken down. And the final point of Qushayrī, which lights the whole sūra: “If ʿAbd al-Muṭṭalib, while still a pagan, was sincere in his recourse to God to defend the House — God did not disappoint his hope, He heard his call. How much more so the believer who calls his Lord — he is not turned away. He was answered because he did not ask for himself, but for the House. And what is for God does not perish.”

mots.def: jaʿala: to render, to make, to transform.
mots.gram: perfect verb + suffix pronoun, 3rd pers. masc. sing.

mots.def: ʿaṣf: tips of seedlings, husks of cereals; chaff before maturity.
maʾkūl: passive participle of akala (to eat); chewed, broken down.
